title: श्री विरूपाक्ष स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री विरूपाक्ष स्तोत्रम् ॥

ॐ नमः शिवाय विरूपाक्षाय।
पम्पातीरनिवासिने हम्पीक्षेत्रवासिने॥

विरूपाक्षं महादेवं पम्पातीरनिवासिनम्।
हेमकूटगिरिस्थं तं प्रणमामि सदाशिवम्॥ १॥

विरूपनेत्रं भगवन्तमीशं
तृतीयनेत्राग्निदहनप्रभावम्।
कामान्तकं लोकहिताय दक्षं
विरूपाक्षं प्रणमामि शम्भुम्॥ २॥

तुङ्गभद्रातटे रम्ये किष्किन्धागिरिसन्निधौ।
पम्पासरोवरतीरे विरूपाक्षो विराजते॥ ३॥

विद्यारण्यमुनीन्द्रेण सेवितं पूजितं सदा।
हरिहरराजसंरक्षितं क्षेत्रमतिपावनम्॥ ४॥

पार्वतीवल्लभं शान्तं कैलासपतिमव्ययम्।
भस्मोद्धूलितसर्वाङ्गं विरूपाक्षं नमाम्यहम्॥ ५॥

नन्दिवाहनमारूढं वृषभध्वजसंयुतम्।
गजचर्मपरीधानं सर्पभूषणभूषितम्॥ ६॥

त्रिपुरान्तकमीशानं कालकालं महेश्वरम्।
मृत्युञ्जयं जगद्योनिं विरूपाक्षं भजाम्यहम्॥ ७॥

हम्पीक्षेत्रे महापुण्ये विजयनगरोत्तमे।
मातङ्गशृङ्गसन्निध्ये ऋष्यमूकसमीपतः॥ ८॥

सुग्रीवस्य सहायं यत्र रामो रघूत्तमः।
पम्पातीर्थे शिवं ध्यात्वा प्रसन्नं तं महेश्वरम्॥ ९॥

पम्पाक्षेत्रसमीपस्थं विरूपाक्षं शिवं हरम्।
प्रत्यक्षं ज्योतिरूपं तं प्रणमामि पुनः पुनः॥ १०॥

विरूपाक्षस्य माहात्म्यं यः पठेद् भक्तिसंयुतः।
सर्वपापविनिर्मुक्तः शिवसायुज्यमाप्नुयात्॥ ११॥

॥ इति श्री विरूपाक्ष स्तोत्रम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Virupaksha Stotram ||

Om Namah Shivaya Virupakshaya |
Pampa-tira-nivasine Hampi-kshetra-vasine ||

Virupaksham Mahadevam Pampa-tira-nivasinam |
Hemakuta-giri-stham tam pranamami Sadashivam || 1 ||

Virupa-netram Bhagavantam-Isham
Tritiya-netra-agni-dahana-prabhavam |
Kamantakam loka-hitaya daksham
Virupaksham pranamami Shambhum || 2 ||

Tungabhadra-tate ramye Kishkindha-giri-sannidhau |
Pampa-sarovara-tire Virupaksho virajate || 3 ||

Vidyaranya-muni-indrena sevitam pujitam sada |
Harihara-raja-samrakshitam kshetram-ati-pavanam || 4 ||

Parvati-vallabham shantam Kailasa-patim-avyayam |
Bhasmoddhulita-sarvangam Virupaksham namamyaham || 5 ||

Nandi-vahanam-arudham Vrishabha-dhvaja-samyutam |
Gajacharma-paridhanam sarpa-bhushana-bhushitam || 6 ||

Tripurantakam-Ishanam Kalakalam Maheshwaram |
Mrityunjayam Jagad-yonim Virupaksham bhajamyaham || 7 ||

Hampi-kshetre maha-punye Vijayanagara-uttame |
Matanga-shringa-sannidhye Rishyamuka-samipatah || 8 ||

Sugrivasya sahayam yatra Ramo Raghuttamah |
Pampa-tirthe Shivam dhyatva prasannam tam Maheshwaram || 9 ||

Pampa-kshetra-samipastham Virupaksham Shivam Haram |
Pratyaksham jyoti-rupam tam pranamami punah punah || 10 ||

Virupakshasya mahatmyam yah pathed bhakti-samyutah |
Sarva-papa-vinirmuktah Shiva-sayujyam-apnuyat || 11 ||

|| Iti Shri Virupaksha Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: पम्पा तीर पर विराजमान, हेमकूट गिरि स्थित, सदाशिव विरूपाक्ष महादेव को प्रणाम। विरूप नेत्र वाले, तृतीय नेत्र की अग्नि से कामदहन करने वाले, लोकहितकारी शम्भु को नमन। तुङ्गभद्रा के रम्य तट पर, किष्किन्धा गिरि के समीप, पम्पा सरोवर तीरे विरूपाक्ष विराजते हैं। विद्यारण्य मुनि सेवित, हरिहर राजा संरक्षित यह अतिपावन क्षेत्र है। त्रिपुरान्तक, कालकाल, मृत्युञ्जय, जगद्योनि विरूपाक्ष को भजन। हम्पी क्षेत्र में जहाँ राम ने सुग्रीव की सहायता की — वहाँ प्रत्यक्ष ज्योतिरूप शिव को पुनः पुनः प्रणाम।
benefits: Invokes the merit of pilgrimage to hampi virupaksha temple
Traditionally recited for removal of all sins and purifies the devotee
Invokes the blessing of divine vision and spiritual wisdom
Connects with the sacred vibrations of Kishkindha Kshetra
description: Virupaksha Stotram — श्री विरूपाक्ष स्तोत्रम् — is a devotional hymn of praise offered to Lord Shiva. This sacred composition is sourced from Shiva Purana and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Regular recitation is believed to connect with the sacred vibrations of Kishkindha Kshetra, grant Shiva-sayujya (union with Lord Shiva), and destroy all sins and purify the devotee. This sacred text is ideally recited on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar for enhanced blessings. At 11 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 8 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Virupaksha Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Virupaksha Stotram in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Sacred hymn to Lord Shiva at Hampi, the ancient Vijayanagara temple.

seoFaqs.question: What does the name Virupaksha mean and why is it given to Shiva?
seoFaqs.answer: Virupaksha literally means 'the one with oblique or unusual eyes' (vi-rupa = irregular form, aksha = eye), referring to Shiva's third eye on the forehead, which is unique among the gods. Wikipedia's Virupaksha Temple article notes this is the form of Shiva venerated as the family deity (kuladevata) of the Vijayanagara emperors. The third eye, opened only to destroy ignorance and the demon Kama, makes Virupaksha the patron of inner vision and royal protection — fitting attributes for an imperial tutelary deity. (Source: Wikipedia Virupaksha Temple Hampi.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Virupaksha at Hampi historically significant?
seoFaqs.answer: The Virupaksha temple at Hampi is one of the few Vijayanagara-era temples still in continuous worship since its founding in the 7th century. It served as the principal religious centre of the Vijayanagara Empire (1336–1646) and as the spiritual axis around which the city's grand bazaars, palaces, and water tanks were planned. Even after the empire fell at the Battle of Talikota (1565) and Hampi was abandoned, the Virupaksha sanctum alone continued daily worship. The Virupaksha Stotram was central to this royal Shaiva tradition. (Source: Wikipedia Virupaksha Temple; Karnataka.com Hampi Virupaksha.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Pampa Devi and how is she connected to the Virupaksha Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Pampa Devi is the consort of Virupaksha at Hampi and the goddess of the local Tungabhadra river (anciently called Pampa). The very name 'Hampi' is a Kannada vernacularisation of 'Pampa-kshetra,' the field of Pampa Devi. The Virupaksha Stotram, in its Vijayanagara recension, includes verses praising Pampa-pati (the lord of Pampa) alongside Virupaksha, making it as much a hymn to the divine couple as to Shiva alone. The Pampa-Virupaksha marriage is annually re-enacted in the temple's December chariot festival. (Source: Karnataka Tourism Hampi Virupaksha; Vijayanagara District website.)

seoFaqs.question: Which Vijayanagara king expanded the Virupaksha temple complex?
seoFaqs.answer: While the original sanctum dates to the 7th century, the present temple's massive nine-storey eastern gopura (50-metre tower), inner courts, and ornate hall (rangamantapa) were commissioned by Lakkan Dandesha, a chieftain (nayaka) under King Deva Raya II in the early 15th century. King Krishnadevaraya (r. 1509–1529), the most celebrated Vijayanagara emperor, added the central rangamantapa in 1510 to commemorate his coronation. Inscriptions in this hall record royal recitations of the Virupaksha Stotram on his coronation day. (Source: Wikipedia Virupaksha Temple; Templenet Virupaksha Hampi.)

seoFaqs.question: What is unique about the daily worship at Virupaksha temple?
seoFaqs.answer: Virupaksha temple maintains a temple elephant (currently Lakshmi, succeeding the famous Rani) who blesses devotees in the courtyard each morning — a continuation of the royal Vijayanagara practice of housing temple elephants. The sanctum also hosts a remarkable inverted shadow of the gopura on the inner wall through a pinhole-camera effect, considered miraculous by devotees. The Virupaksha Stotram is chanted at the daily noon arati and again at the Shejarati (night closing) ceremony. (Source: Karnataka.com Virupaksha Temple; Incredible India Hampi Virupaksha.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the annual Virupaksha festival and what role does the stotram play?
seoFaqs.answer: The temple celebrates the Virupaksha-Pampa Kalyanotsavam (divine wedding) in February, the Phalapuja chariot festival in December, and the Mahashivaratri night vigil. During the chariot festival, devotees pull the great wooden ratha through the Hampi Bazaar street, with the Virupaksha Stotram recited continuously by temple priests and thousands of pilgrims. The temple's UNESCO World Heritage status (since 1986, as part of the 'Group of Monuments at Hampi') has revived public interest in these stotra-recitation traditions. (Source: Vijayanagara District tourism page; UNESCO World Heritage Hampi.)
